data.title: Downtown Las Vegas casino owner gets creative in trying to convince Canadians to return
data.description:
Casino owner Derek Stevens has a plan to lure Canadians back to Las Vegas following a weak year of tourism trends from the nation that typically sends the most traffic to the gambling mecca.

Stevens, who owns the Golden Gate Hotel & Casino, The D Las Vegas, and Circa Resort & Casino in downtown Las Vegas, said his properties will accept Canadian dollars on a par with the U.S. dollar, which will give Canadians a significant discount at the current exchange rate of $1.00 U.S. dollar to $0.73 Canadian dollar. The promotion will run until August 31 and applies to hotel rooms, bars at the three properties, and $500 worth of casino play. Notably, Circa Resorts & Casino opened in October 2020 as the first new hotel-casino built from the ground up in downtown Las Vegas in roughly four decades, and it is positioned as a modern homage to classic Las Vegas history, as well as a value play for tourists in comparison to Strip properties.

Underpinning the appeal to the Great White North, Canadian tourism to Las Vegas has slumped sharply over the past year. The Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority estimated Canadian visitation fell close to 25% in 2024, while overall tourism to Las Vegas was down about 7%. Airline seat capacity from Canadian cities to Las Vegas has declined around 40% to the lowest level since about 2006. The biggest factor cited in recent surveys was resentment against U.S. policies toward Canada. Other factors included value perception of Las Vegas trips, the exchange rate, and travel expenses in general.

MGM Resorts (MGM) and Caesars Entertainment (CZR) are the two biggest casino operators in Las Vegas.

data.title: Ted Sarandos to testify at a February Senate hearing on the Netflix-Warner deal
data.description:
Netflix Inc. (NFLX) co-chief executive Ted Sarandos is planning to testify in February in front of a U.S. Senate committee that is scrutinizing the company's $82.7B deal for the streaming and studio assets of Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (WBD), Bloomberg reported on Thursday.

Bruce Campbell, the strategy chief at Warner Bros., is also planning to appear, sources told Bloomberg. Executives from Paramount Skydance Corp. (PSKY) aren’t expected to testify at the hearing.

While Netflix is expected to win the bidding war for Warner's assets, its revised $27.75 per share all-cash offer is considered more complicated from an antitrust perspective due to the streaming giant’s dominant position in subscription video-on-demand across Europe.

Paramount, with strong ties to the current Trump administration, is not backing down and is relentlessly pursuing the HBO parent's coveted assets through a $30 per share hostile takeover bid.

Senior executives from the David Ellison-run media company have held talks with French President Emmanuel Macron and discussed with UK officials as part of a European charm offensive to gain support for their offer.

Even U.S. President Donald Trump recently voiced his support for a Paramount-Warner Bros. deal on his social media platform.

While Hollywood critics have expressed concerns about the impact of a Netflix-WBD deal, Sarandos, in an extensive interview published in The New York Times last week, reassured that the theatrical industry will not be impacted.

data.title: Trump accuses JPMorgan, Dimon of debanking in $5B lawsuit - report (update)
data.description:
Update at 1:45 PM ET: adds JPMorgan Chase's statement regarding Trump's lawsuit in the fourth paragraph.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday is filing a $5B lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and its longtime CEO Jamie Dimon, alleging the bank cut ties with him and his businesses for political reasons, according to a media report.

The suit alleges JPMorgan (JPM) committed trade libel and breached its implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, Fox Business reported, citing the complaint, which was filed in Miami-Dade County state court. Trump also alleged that Dimon ran afoul of Florida's Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

"We set high expectations and hold ourselves accountable. We do the right thing—not necessarily the easy or expedient thing. We abide by the letter and spirit of the laws and regulations everywhere we do business and have zero tolerance for unethical behavior," the lawsuit said, per JPMorgan's (JPM) code of conduct, Fox Business reported. "Despite claiming to hold these principles dear, JPMC violated them by unilaterally—and without warning or remedy—terminating several of Plaintiff’s bank accounts."

In response to the complaint, JPMorgan (JPM) stated that "the suit has no merit," as the bank "does not close accounts for political or religious reasons." Rather, accounts get closed when "they create legal or regulatory risk for the company," it said. "We regret having to do so, but often rules and regulatory expectations lead us to do so. We have been asking both this Administration and prior administrations to change the rules and regulations that put us in this position, and we support this Administration’s efforts to prevent the weaponization of the banking sector.” (Added at 1:45 p.m. ET)

The White House didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

JPMorgan (JPM), the U.S.'s largest bank, said in November it's facing investigations linked to Trump's order targeting lenders accused of restricting access for political or ideological reasons. Separately, his privately-owned conglomerate in March sued Capital One Financial (COF), accusing it of wrongfully terminating more than 300 company accounts in 2021.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Market Voices: Trump sues JPMorgan, Lutnick on Canada, SpaceX IPO
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • President Trump and other plaintiffs are suing JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and CEO Jamie Dimon for $5B in damages for allegedly de-banking them following the Jan. 6, 2021 riot on Capitol Hill.

“Plaintiffs are confident that JPMC’s unilateral decision came about as a result of political and social motivations and JPMC’s unsubstantiated, ‘woke’ beliefs that it needed to distance itself from President Trump and his conservative political views,” the complaint asserts, according to CNBC.

The complaint, which was filed in Florida, added that JPMorgan's “reckless decision is leading a growing trend by financial institutions in the United States of America to cut off a consumer’s access to banking services if their political views contradict with those of the financial institution.”

In a statement, JPMorgan told CNBC that it believes the suit has no merit.

“We respect the President’s right to sue us and our right to defend ourselves—that's what courts are for,” said JPMorgan spokeswoman Patricia Wexler. “JPMC does not close accounts for political or religious reasons.”

  • U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said that negotiations for the U.S.-Mexico-Canada, or USMCA, trade deal would likely start this summer and could be impacted by Canada's recent dealings with China.

In an interview, Lutnick criticized Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney's recent comments that China was a more predictable trading partner than the U.S., asserting that Canada had the best trading deal with the U.S. after Mexico. 

“We should look at it as just political noise coming out of a prime minister,” Lutnick said in an interview with Bloomberg in Davos. “I don’t think it can be real, because he took out the math of Canada’s economy and doing business with the United States of America’s $30T economy. There’s no such thing as changing what they have today.”

Lutnick also questioned the wisdom of Canada pursuing closer trade relations with China, such as the importation of Chinese EVs.

“Do you think the president of the United States is going to say you should keep having the second-best deal in the world?" Lutnick quipped.

Lutnick added that negotiations for the USMCA trade deal could begin in mid-summer. 

  • SpaceX (SPACE) has already lined up four banks for an IPO that could value the company at around $1.5T.

The banks so far include Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Morgan Stanley (MS). SpaceX hopes to hold the IPO later this year and raise “significantly more” than $30B, according to Bloomberg, which cited the Financial Times. 

SpaceX (SPACE), which owns Starlink (STRLK), is controlled by Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk.

data.title: Atlantic Union projects $29B–$30B year-end loan balances in 2026 while expanding North Carolina footprint
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Asbury, President and CEO, opened by stating Atlantic Union Bankshares reported "strong fourth quarter financial results, reflecting disciplined execution and successful integration of the Sandy Spring's acquisition." Asbury emphasized, "While merger-related charges continue to affect this quarter's results, the underlying operating performance supports our continued confidence in achieving the strategic goals associated with the Sandy Spring acquisition."
  • Asbury noted, "Quarterly loan growth was approximately 6.3% annualized, ending the year at $27.8 billion. Our pipelines were higher at the end of the fourth quarter than they were at the start of the quarter, which suggests we are on track for loan growth consistent with our full year 2026 outlook."
  • Asbury highlighted the completion of the core systems conversion in October and stated, "Only modest residual merger-related expenses [are] anticipated in the first quarter, we expect the noise associated with our merger-related expenses to decline."
  • Asbury reiterated the focus on organic growth opportunities and described the markets as "among the most attractive in the country," adding, "With disciplined execution of our prior acquisitions and no additional acquisitions currently planned during this phase of our strategic plan, our focus now shifts to demonstrating the franchise's earnings power and capital generation ability."
  • Robert Gorman, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "In the fourth quarter, reported net income available to common shareholders was $109 million and earnings per common share were $0.77."
  • Gorman added, "Adjusted operating earnings available to common shareholders were $138.4 million or $0.97 per common share in the fourth quarter, resulting in an adjusted operating return on tangible common equity of 22.1%, and adjusted operating return on assets of 1.5% and an adjusted operating efficiency ratio of 47.8% in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Gorman reiterated, "We are maintaining our full year 2026 financial outlook for AUB that was provided at our Investor Day in December. We expect loan balances to end the year between $29 billion and $30 billion, while year-end deposit balances are projected to be between $31.5 billion and $32.5 billion."
  • Gorman indicated, "The allowance for credit losses to loan balances is projected to remain at current levels in the 115 to 120 basis point range and the net charge-off ratio is expected to fall between 10 and 15 basis points in 2026."
  • Net interest income for 2026 is projected to be between $1.350 billion and $1.375 billion, with the fully tax equivalent net interest margin expected to fall between 3.90% and 4% for the full year.
  • Gorman stated, "Noninterest income is expected to be between $220 million and $230 million, while adjusted operating noninterest expense is expected to fall in the range of $750 million to $760 million, which includes the expense impact of our North Carolina investment and other 2026 strategic initiatives."

Financial Results

  • Net charge-offs decreased to $916,000 or 1 basis point annualized in the fourth quarter from $38.6 million or 56 basis points annualized in the third quarter.
  • Tax equivalent net interest income was $334.8 million, which was an increase of $11.2 million from the third quarter, driven by a decrease in interest expense and increases in interest income on loans held for investment and the securities portfolio.
  • The fourth quarter's tax equivalent net interest margin increased 13 basis points from the prior quarter to 3.96%.
  • Noninterest income increased $5.2 million to $57 million for the fourth quarter from $51.8 million in the prior quarter, primarily driven by the absence of a prior quarter pretax loss related to the settlement of CRE loans.
  • Adjusted operating noninterest expense increased $1.4 million to $186.9 million for the fourth quarter, primarily due to an increase in noncredit-related losses on customer transactions and higher marketing and advertising expense.
  • At December 31, total deposits were $30.5 billion, a decrease of $193.7 million or 2.5% annualized from the prior quarter.
  • During the fourth quarter, a common stock dividend of $0.37 per share was paid, an increase of 8.8% from the third quarter.
  • Tangible book value per common share increased approximately 4% to $19.69 per share in the fourth quarter.

Q&A

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about the 2026 guidance range for net interest income and deposit betas. Gorman explained, "We're guiding to net interest income between $1.35 billion and $1.375 billion. To come on the higher end of that, it's really going to depend on... elevated accretion income as we saw this quarter... Also, can we continue to lower deposit costs as the Fed reduces the Fed funds rate."
  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Inquired about loan pipeline growth and traction in the Sandy Spring portfolio. Asbury commented, "We had a modest increase in the total pipeline by end of year, end of quarter versus beginning of quarter... The feedback we're getting from our market leaders is quite encouraging."
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Questioned loan pricing and deposit costs. Gorman stated, "We're seeing about [6% to 6.20%] loan pricing... In terms of the deposit cost at the end of December... it was about 1.96%."
  • Brian Wilczynski, Morgan Stanley: Asked about focus areas for organic loan growth. David Ring, EVP, responded, "We've seen the Sandy Spring part of the franchise really turned the corner... North Carolina, steady as she goes there... specialty businesses actually contributed largely to some of the growth we've had in the fourth quarter."
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on cost saves from the Sandy Spring deal. Gorman replied, "About $80 million is what we had projected, 27.5%. In the numbers, it's probably about... $60 million. We're getting another $5 million coming out of the fourth quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, seeking clarifications on guidance ranges and drivers of growth, and frequently requesting details on potential upside and risk factors.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, with Asbury stating, "We believe we are now seeing clear tangible benefits from these efforts," and Gorman frequently reiterating guidance and expressing confidence in achieving strategic goals.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management demonstrated increased confidence, especially regarding merger integration and loan pipeline growth. Analyst sentiment remained consistent, focused on forward-looking metrics and operational execution, though questions were more positive on pipeline growth and merger synergies than prior quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan growth in Q4 accelerated substantially to 6.3% annualized from 0.5% annualized in Q3, with management highlighting a stronger pipeline and record production.
  • Net interest margin increased to 3.96% from 3.83% in the prior quarter, while adjusted operating efficiency ratio improved slightly.
  • Deposit balances decreased at a similar pace to Q3, attributed to seasonal patterns and post-acquisition adjustments.
  • Cost saves from Sandy Spring acquisition progressed, with additional benefits expected to be realized in Q1 2026.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to more assertive confidence in Q4, especially about demonstrating earnings power and capital generation.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on expense management, loan growth sustainability, and integration progress, but Q4 saw increased questions on North Carolina expansion and organic growth opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Noncredit-related losses on customer transactions, mainly fraud-related, contributed to higher expenses in the quarter. Management described these as "episodic," with no anticipated persistent impact.
  • Market uncertainty and economic headwinds continue to pose loan growth forecasting challenges, though management expects these to remain manageable.
  • Seasonal deposit fluctuations and post-acquisition deposit remixing noted as areas to monitor, though management expects stabilization.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Atlantic Union Bankshares enters 2026 with momentum from a pivotal year, citing strong loan growth, merger integration progress, and improved operating metrics. With most merger-related noise receding, the company is positioned to demonstrate its earnings power, pursue organic growth across diversified markets, and achieve its projected financial targets for 2026, including loan balances between $29 billion and $30 billion. The combination of operational discipline, enhanced scale from the Sandy Spring acquisition, and targeted expansion in North Carolina underpins management’s confidence in delivering sustainable, top-tier performance and long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Equity Bancshares targets over $5 EPS in 2026 as assets surge 50% and Frontier merger integrates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Equity Bancshares, Inc. (EQBK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Brad Elliott, Founder, Chairman & CEO, highlighted the company’s rapid expansion, stating, "We started the year with $5.3 billion in assets and finished with $6.4 billion in assets. We added an additional $1.4 billion when we closed the Frontier merger on January 1. That's nearly 50% growth." Elliott set an ambitious goal, saying, "With that kind of scale, we're pushing to earn more than $5 per share in 2026."
  • Elliott emphasized the company’s focus on technology and efficiency, crediting the tech team for "a big push on using technology to improve service and efficiency. We're focused on using data smarter and moving faster across the board."
  • Chris Navratil, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "Last night, we reported net income of $22.1 million or $1.15 per diluted share. Adjusting for noncore items... adjusted earnings were $23.3 million or $1.21 per diluted share compared to adjusted earnings of $22.4 million or $1.17 per diluted share in the previous quarter."
  • Navratil detailed, "Net interest income for the quarter was $63.5 million, up $1 million linked quarter. Margin for the quarter was 4.47%, an improvement of 2 basis points when compared to margin of 4.45% linked quarter."
  • Richard Sems, CEO & Director, described credit improvement: "Nonaccrual loans moved down to $40.3 million from $48.6 million linked quarter, a 17% decline."

Outlook

  • Management projects, "We're pushing to earn more than $5 per share in 2026."
  • Navratil indicated, "We anticipate margin for the quarter and throughout 2026 of 4.2% to 4.35%."
  • The addition of Frontier is expected to add noninterest expense of $23 million to $24 million and noninterest income of $2 million to $3 million.
  • The company expects loan-to-deposit ratio of 88% and loans as a percentage of average earning assets of approximately 80% for Q1 2026.

Financial Results

  • Net income for the quarter was $22.1 million or $1.15 per diluted share, with adjusted earnings at $23.3 million or $1.21 per diluted share.
  • Net interest income rose to $63.5 million, and noninterest income reached $9.5 million.
  • Noninterest expenses for the quarter were $46.6 million, with adjusted noninterest expenses at $44.1 million.
  • The margin for the quarter stood at 4.47%. Tangible common equity (TCE) closed the quarter at 9.9%, while CET1 and total capital were 13.1% and 16.3%, respectively.
  • The company repurchased 172,338 shares at a weighted average cost of $41.69. 872,662 shares remained under authorization.

Q&A

  • Ryan Payne, D.A. Davidson, inquired about margin guidance and inclusion of Frontier accretion. Elliott responded, "That does include the accretion for Frontier into 2026."
  • Payne questioned loan growth amid competition. Sems stated, "We're definitely seeing some of that on the competition front. So we just kind of strategically made that decision that we're continuing to hold our pricing higher."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW, asked about loan participations from Frontier. Sems replied, "It's probably $50-ish million in that range across the board of types."
  • Del Monte also asked about expense guidance and cost saves. Navratil said, "The 23% I think, is still a good number...using 23% is still a good number today, and there may be upside to that as well."
  • Del Monte inquired about capital management and M&A. Elliott noted, "We've got plenty of capital to do both, and we feel very confident that we are going to have opportunities to do both along the way."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler, asked for margin starting point post-Frontier. Navratil answered, "I would look at the low end to the midpoint for the first quarter. So let's call it, 4.25% for the first quarter."
  • Race pressed on loan growth cadence. Sems indicated, "The pipeline...is fairly strong, consistent with where it's been in the other quarter."
  • Anya Pelshaw, Hovde Group, questioned deposit competition. Sems said, "Deposit account gathering is really good...balances continue to be challenging because there are a lot of people out there looking for balances."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions around margin sustainability, loan growth under competitive pressure, expense management, and capital deployment, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on guidance and integration impacts.
  • Management’s tone remained confident during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referring to "alignment," "focus," and "confidence" in execution, as well as emphasizing disciplined strategy, stating "we feel very confident that we are going to have opportunities to do both along the way."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence level appears even higher, driven by successful M&A integrations and solid capital positioning, while analysts remain constructively probing but not overtly skeptical.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter reflects a major shift with the closing and integration of the Frontier merger, driving nearly 50% asset growth and expanded geographic reach versus the previous quarter’s focus on NBC integration.
  • Guidance language in Q4 is more explicit, targeting over $5 per share in 2026 and projecting margin range and expense impacts from Frontier.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on deposit costs, loan growth, and M&A, but questions in Q4 increasingly centered on integration synergies, cost saves, and capital allocation.
  • Management’s tone has shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to firm confidence in Q4, underscored by robust performance and successful strategic execution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing competitive pressures in loan pricing and deposit gathering, highlighting the need for discipline in pricing and strategic customer selection.
  • Litigation settlement expense related to overdraft suits is an ongoing risk, with $1 million accrued this quarter.
  • Pipeline strength and loan payoffs remain uncertainties, with Sems stating that payoffs "can do a lot of times come out of the blue."
  • The impact of integrating the Frontier portfolio on credit quality and margin dilution was noted, but management expects it to be manageable.

Final Takeaway

Equity Bancshares concluded a transformational year, achieving nearly 50% asset growth with the Frontier merger and setting an ambitious goal to earn more than $5 per share in 2026. The company enters the new year with a strong capital position, disciplined approach to pricing and growth, and a commitment to operational efficiency and technology-driven service. Management expresses full alignment and confidence in delivering on stated targets and capitalizing on growth opportunities across its expanded footprint.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Third Coast Bancshares outlines $75M–$100M quarterly loan growth target as Keystone merger integration begins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Third Coast Bancshares (TCBX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Bart Caraway, Founder, Chairman, President & CEO, announced significant balance sheet growth in the fourth quarter and full year, stating, "Gross loans increased by $230 million, or 5.5% compared to the third quarter, reaching $4.39 billion. This marks a 10.8% rise compared to the previous year, surpassing our targeted run rate of 8%. Total assets mirrored this upward trend, ending the year at $5.34 billion, reflecting a 5.5% increase over the third quarter, and an 8.1% rise compared to the previous year-end." Deposits also rose, with "over $254 million in the fourth quarter, reaching $4.6 billion, a 5.8% increase from the third quarter, and a 7.3% rise compared to a year ago."
  • Caraway emphasized, "The increase in service charges and fees is noteworthy, with an approximately 24% increase over the third quarter and an impressive 55% year-over-year rise."
  • Caraway noted the company achieved "book value and tangible book value [which] rose to $33.47 and $32.12, respectively, reflecting a year-over-year increase of 16.8% and 17.7%. Our return on average assets remained robust, achieving an annualized 1.33% for the full year of 2025, returning a year-over-year enhancement of more than 26%."
  • John McWhorter, Senior EVP & CFO, reported, "We reported net income of $17.9 million for the fourth quarter, leading to a record total annual net income of $66.3 million, reflecting a 39% increase year-over-year." He added, "Earnings per share exceeded expectations, reaching $1.02 per diluted share for the quarter, and totaling $3.79 for the year, representing a 36% increase from the prior year."
  • McWhorter detailed, "Net interest income was $52.2 million in the fourth quarter and $195.2 million for the year, an increase of 21% from the previous year."
  • Audrey Duncan, Senior EVP & Chief Credit Officer, highlighted, "Nonaccrual loans saw continued improvement for the fourth consecutive quarter, decreasing by $603,000 in the fourth quarter and $16.7 million for the full year."

Outlook

  • Caraway projected, "We also have set our strategic and financial outlook for 2026 including, achieving loan growth targets of $75 million to $100 million per quarter, establishing an annualized growth rate of approximately 8%."
  • The integration of the Keystone Bancshares, Inc. merger is a focal point, with Caraway stating, "Once complete, this strategic partnership will unite us as a combined $6 billion entity with 22 locations across Texas."
  • McWhorter commented on net interest margin, "We believe the core net interest margin was 3.90% for the quarter, up about 10 basis points from the prior quarter."

Financial Results

  • McWhorter confirmed, "Net income of $17.9 million for the fourth quarter" and "earnings per share exceeded expectations, reaching $1.02 per diluted share for the quarter."
  • Net interest income reached $52.2 million for the quarter, while investment securities ended at $575 million, reflecting a $7.5 million decrease during the quarter.
  • McWhorter noted, "Deposits rose by $254 million in the fourth quarter, totaling $4.6 billion."
  • The company maintained a loan-to-deposit ratio of 95%, with a cost of funds at 3.33% in the fourth quarter, improving by 23 basis points from the third quarter.
  • Duncan indicated the allowance for credit losses was 1% of total loans, "a slight decline from 1.02% at the third quarter and previous year-end."

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods: Questioned expense actions and sign-on/severance costs. McWhorter responded, "Legal and professional line item had about $1 million in merger-related expenses. And we think we have another probably $5 million that we'll incur in the next couple of quarters." He added, "For salary and employee benefits, it was more in the hundreds of thousands of range as far as kind of nonrecurring type expenses."
  • Lay followed up on hiring post-acquisition. Caraway said, "We're surgical and selective. But as great talent comes available...we're going to continue to add them...no fundamental shift as much as ongoing operations."
  • Lay asked about loan growth post-Keystone. Caraway stated, "I still think that's the right growth rate. So notice we've kind of bumped up the lower number a little bit just because we're seeing such good pipeline growth."
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about C&I loan growth and margin. Caraway described the $75 million to $100 million range as "the base case," explaining, "I feel comfortable with where we stand right now and projecting forward that we're going to hit those numbers."
  • Rose queried margin sustainability. McWhorter explained, "We had about $1.5 million of what we think of excess loan fees...there's nothing I'm aware of today that would be similar in the first quarter. So I would expect the margin to be back down into the 3.90% range."
  • Rose inquired about ongoing M&A appetite. Caraway replied, "We're certainly focused on consummating the Keystone deal and integrating them...The M&A side for us is just ongoing part of our strategic planning."
  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: Asked if deposit growth was driven by campaigns. McWhorter stated, "No, Bernie, this is somewhat seasonal for us...It wasn't anything special that we were doing."
  • Von Gizycki asked about expense growth in 2026. McWhorter projected, "We're probably going to be plus 5%, maybe 6% or 7%, but we still think our revenue growth is going to be quite a bit more than that."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked about future securitization. McWhorter replied, "I do think it's likely that we'll do another one this year. I think that it would look a little bit different than the ones that we've done in the past."
  • Olney inquired about the Keystone merger timeline. Caraway said, "It's going along as planned."
  • David Storms, Stonegate: Asked about dynamic pricing to lower interest expense. Caraway responded, "With the new system, the core system, we have better pricing tools, better ways to understand our customers...I think we are well positioned for any kind of rate changes that are there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on expense clarity, margin sustainability, and M&A, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with a focus on execution risk and future growth consistency.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing stable growth, operational discipline, and the ability to deliver future value. Caraway said, "We remain focused on proven banking model that supports sustainable growth, strong profitability and long-term value for our shareholders."
  • The tone was broadly consistent with the previous quarter, with both analysts and management showing steady confidence but probing more closely into post-merger integration and ongoing expense management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for quarterly loan growth shifted upward, with a new range of $75 million to $100 million per quarter compared to $50 million to $100 million previously.
  • Strategic focus intensified on the Keystone merger integration and operational efficiency initiatives, compared to the previous quarter's emphasis on record-setting asset growth and fee income momentum.
  • Analysts' questions shifted more toward expense management, merger-related costs, and fee income sustainability.
  • Key metrics such as nonaccrual loans, net income, and book value improved sequentially.
  • Management's sentiment remained confident, though more attention was given to integration execution and growth scalability.

Risks and Concerns

  • McWhorter pointed to $1 million in merger-related expenses this quarter, with $5 million more expected in the coming quarters.
  • Management acknowledged that loan fees are likely to be "lumpy" and that the core margin could decrease without one-time events.
  • Caraway noted that growth can be "lumpy," and that while the pipeline is strong, "there's still uncertainty in the market."
  • Expense growth is expected to be higher in the first half of 2026, but management reiterated that revenue growth should outpace expense increases.

Final Takeaway

Third Coast Bancshares closed 2025 with strong balance sheet expansion, robust earnings, and improved asset quality, while setting a new loan growth target of $75 million to $100 million per quarter for 2026. The upcoming integration of Keystone Bancshares is expected to further strengthen the company’s Texas footprint and operational scale. Management emphasized ongoing operational discipline and selective hiring, as well as continued investment in efficiency and risk management, positioning the company for sustainable growth and shareholder value in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BitGo Holdings stock pops 20%+ in trading debut
data.description:
BitGo Holdings (BTGO) stock jumped 25% in its opening trade as a public company on Thursday. The stock spiked as high as 36% in the first half hour of trading. By 1:30 PM ET, its gain moderated to 18% to $21.20.

The digital asset infrastructure company offered 11M shares at $18 apiece in its initial public offering, raising ~$198M in gross proceeds. Selling shareholders offered 821,595 shares in the IPO.

The IPO valued the firm at ~$2.08B.

data.title: Higher spending on subscriptions, mobile content, and hardware drives U.S. videogame sales in 2025
data.description:
Total U.S. video game sales rose 3% for the month of December from last year, while sales figures for 2025 are up 1%, according to a research report from Circana on Thursday.

December sales came in at $7.77B compared with $7.57B for the same period last year.

The bulk of sales, as usual, was generated by game content, which rose 3% to $5.93B for the month. The growth was led by a 24% jump in subscription spending and a 6% increase in PC, cloud, and non-console VR content. Spending on console and mobile content was flat year-on-year.

Hardware sales rose 6% to $1.21B for the month, while accessory sales saw a 7% decline from the prior year to $625M.

For 2025, overall U.S. sales were $60.67B vs. $59.88B in 2024. Game content sales were up 1% at $52.30B, hardware sales rose 9% to $5.44B, while accessory sales fell 7% to $2.95B.

When looking closely at hardware numbers, Circana analyst Mat Piscatella noted that Nintendo Switch 2 led the hardware market in both unit and dollar sales during December and for 2025, helping offset year-on-year declines across other platforms. PlayStation 5 (SONY) came in second across both measures and time periods.

Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 was December’s best-selling game across physical & tracked digital spending, the report said. Battlefield 6 finished as 2025’s best-selling video game in tracked full-game dollar sales. Fortnite led in total active users across both PlayStation and Xbox platforms during 2025. Roblox was 2025’s leading publisher in digital at retail spending for both December and 2025.

The month's top 10 mobile games by U.S. consumer spending are MONOPOLY GO!, Last War: Survival, Royal Match, Candy Crush Saga (NASDAQ:MSFT), Kingshot, Gossip Harbor, Clash Royale, Whiteout Survival, Royal Kingdom, and Last Z: Survival.

"Out with the old, in with the new. With the exception of Gossip Harbor, newer games are taking spots away from legacy titles," said Sam Aune, the gaming insight analyst at Sensor Tower. "With Last Z: Survival cracking the top 10, now 4 out of the top 10 mobile games by US in-app purchase revenue are part of the new wave of 4X strategy games from Chinese publishers taking over mobile gaming."

Related tickers: OTCPK:NTDOY, SONY, MSFT, TBCH, LOGI, CRSR, EA, TTWO, WBD, OTCPK:UBSFY, OTCPK:NCBDY, OTCPK:SQNNY, OTCPK:CCOEY, OTCPK:SGAMY, OTCPK:KONMY, OTCPK:NEXOY, SKLZ, SE, NTES, OTCPK:TCEHY, RBLX.

Retail stock: GME

ETFs: GAMR, ESPO, NERD

data.title: Trump's $5B lawsuit against JPM is just 10 days’ worth of its revenue
data.description:
The $5B lawsuit filed Thursday by President Donald Trump against JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and CEO Jamie Dimon represents roughly 10 days of the bank’s revenue, according to a financial analysis.

Trump alleges the nation’s largest bank terminated his accounts for political reasons, seeking damages that amount to approximately 7.1 days of JPM’s average daily revenue based on the bank’s massive financial output.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) generates approximately $507M in revenue every day, calculated from an annual net revenue of roughly $185B for 2025, according to the bank’s full-year financial results.

The institution’s operational scale is staggering – J.P. Morgan Payments processes over $10T globally in payments each day. This daily revenue figure represents an increase from 2024, when the bank generated approximately $493M per day based on total annual net revenue of roughly $180.6B.

The suit, filed in Miami-Dade County state court, alleges JPMorgan (JPM) committed trade libel and breached its implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, Fox Business reported. Trump also alleged that Dimon ran afoul of Florida’s Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

JPMorgan denies the claims, stating accounts are not closed based on political beliefs, and the bank has indicated it will fight the suit in court.

The lawsuit comes amid a broader context. The bank said in November it faces investigations linked to Trump’s order targeting lenders accused of restricting access for political or ideological reasons, according to media reports.

Bank ETFs: (KBWB), (KBE), (FTXO)

Regional Bank ETFs: (KRE), (KBWR), (IAT), (DPST)
